Coffee Field Model: Sequential Action Planning

Source: vignettes/coffee_field_model.Rmd
coffee_field_model.Rmd

What Is a Functional Field?

A functional field is a model of how situational forces propagate through an environment to produce action outcomes. At each moment the field specifies which forces are afforded — i.e., which causal pathways are open given the current state of the situation. Forces spread step-by-step through the network: disturbances at time t are transmitted through the field matrix to produce new disturbances at time t + 1, which then accumulate into the situation.

This vignette walks through a concrete example — making a cup of drip coffee — to show how a four-step action sequence becomes causally connected through a field model. The key insight is causal gating: later steps are only afforded once earlier steps have changed the situation. A plan adds intentional impulses that drive the sequence forward, but the outcome of each step is what opens up the next pathway.

Step 1: Define the Layout

The layout is a character matrix where each non-zero cell encodes one node as "type_name". See ?prepField for the full list of type codes; the ones used here are:

Code Meaning
a Appraisal (left-facing triangle)
o Object — binary present / absent (diamond)
d Do / decision action (right-facing triangle)

The “4 step DC to mouth” layout uses a staircase arrangement: each action–outcome pair steps one row up and two columns to the right. This diagonal flow naturally represents the sequential, irreversible character of the process — you cannot sip until you have poured, pour until it has brewed, etc. The motivational prerequisites (Want(Co).p, H(Ings).p) and the terminal utility appraisal (U.p) are placed in the rightmost column.

library(funfield)

coffee_layout <- matrix(0, nrow = 5, ncol = 15)

# --- Staircase: bottom-left to top-right ---
coffee_layout[5,  1] <- "o_s1"              # combined readiness state
coffee_layout[5,  3] <- "d_make(s2).p"      # action: set up the drip coffeemaker
coffee_layout[4,  4] <- "o_s2"              # coffeemaker is set up and ready
coffee_layout[4,  6] <- "d_turn(ON.DC).p"   # action: turn on the drip coffeemaker
coffee_layout[3,  7] <- "o_H(Co).Pot"       # coffee has brewed into the pot
coffee_layout[3,  9] <- "d_pour(Pot,Cup).p" # action: pour from pot into cup
coffee_layout[2, 10] <- "o_H(Co).Cup"       # coffee is in the cup
coffee_layout[2, 12] <- "d_sip(Cup).p"      # action: sip from the cup
coffee_layout[1, 13] <- "o_H(Co).p"         # having / enjoyed the coffee

# --- Right column: motivational inputs and terminal utility ---
coffee_layout[1, 15] <- "a_U.p"             # appraisal: utility / satisfaction
coffee_layout[4, 15] <- "o_Want(Co).p"      # wanting coffee
coffee_layout[5, 15] <- "o_H(Ings).p"       # having the necessary ingredients

fprops <- prepField(coffee_layout)

# Full English display labels (internal names → readable text)
coffee_labels <- c(
  "s1"              = "Ready to\nmake coffee",
  "make(s2).p"      = "Set up\ncoffeemaker",
  "s2"              = "Coffeemaker\nready",
  "turn(ON.DC).p"   = "Turn on\ncoffeemaker",
  "H(Co).Pot"       = "Coffee\nin pot",
  "pour(Pot,Cup).p" = "Pour into\ncup",
  "H(Co).Cup"       = "Coffee\nin cup",
  "sip(Cup).p"      = "Sip from\ncup",
  "H(Co).p"         = "Have\ncoffee",
  "U.p"             = "Satisfied",
  "Want(Co).p"      = "Want\ncoffee",
  "H(Ings).p"       = "Have\ningredients"
)

prepField() strips the type prefix and returns fprops, a list that bundles the processed layout, node names, shapes, sizes, and an empty field matrix (fprops$fmat) ready to be filled with paths.

You can optionally inspect or adjust the layout interactively:

# Opens a drag-and-drop editor — not run during vignette build
fieldEditor(coffee_layout)

Step 2: Define the Field Function

ff() computes the force-transmission matrix ft from the current situation vector s. Two kinds of paths populate ft:

  1. Afforded paths — only present when a prerequisite situation node is active. For example, the path make(s2).p → s2 is only afforded while s["s1"] > 0. If the prerequisite is absent, the path weight is zero and no force flows — the gate is closed.

  2. Planned paths — the agent’s goal-directed intention to act at each state. These are supplied by the plan argument and added on top of afforded paths.

ff <- function(s, plan = DoNothing) {
  ft <- fprops$fmat   # start with the zero matrix (names already set)

  # --- Afforded paths (gated by situation state) ---

  # Gate 1: s1 present → setting up the coffeemaker produces s2
  if (s["s1"] > 0) {
    ft["make(s2).p", "s2"] <- 0.9
  }

  # Gate 2: coffeemaker ready (s2) → turning it on brews coffee into the pot
  if (s["s2"] > 0) {
    ft["turn(ON.DC).p", "H(Co).Pot"] <- 0.9
  }

  # Gate 3: coffee in the pot → pouring fills the cup
  if (s["H(Co).Pot"] > 0) {
    ft["pour(Pot,Cup).p", "H(Co).Cup"] <- 0.9
  }

  # Gate 4: coffee in the cup → sipping produces the "have coffee" state
  if (s["H(Co).Cup"] > 0) {
    ft["sip(Cup).p", "H(Co).p"] <- 0.9
  }

  # Terminal: having coffee produces utility
  if (s["H(Co).p"] > 0) {
    ft["H(Co).p", "U.p"] <- 0.9
  }

  # --- Plan forces (intentional action impulses) ---
  # plan() returns a matrix with 1s where the agent intends to act.
  # Adding these forces lets disturbances from situation nodes drive
  # the corresponding action nodes forward.
  ft <- ft + plan(ft)

  list(ft = ft)
}

Why causal gating works here

At time step 1 the situation is [s1=1, Want(Co).p=1, H(Ings).p=1, ...]. Because s["s2"] = 0, the gate for turn(ON.DC).p → H(Co).Pot is closed: that path has weight zero and no force flows through it. Even though the agent plans to turn on the coffeemaker, the outcome of doing so is not yet causally possible — the coffeemaker has not been set up yet. Only once s2 becomes positive (the coffeemaker is ready) does that gate open, and force can propagate to the next stage.

Step 3: Define Plans

A plan is a function that accepts a field matrix and returns a same-shaped matrix marking which paths the agent intends to use (coded as 1). DoNothing returns all zeros; do4step adds one planned impulse per step, connecting each situation outcome to its corresponding next action.

DoNothing <- function(x) { x * 0 }

do4step <- function(x) {
  x[] <- 0
  x["s1",        "make(s2).p"]      <- 1  # in s1: set up the coffeemaker
  x["s2",        "turn(ON.DC).p"]   <- 1  # ready: turn it on
  x["H(Co).Pot", "pour(Pot,Cup).p"] <- 1  # brewed: pour into cup
  x["H(Co).Cup", "sip(Cup).p"]      <- 1  # in cup: sip
  x
}

Step 4: Set Up the Initial Situation and Run the Simulation

The initial situation s is all zeros. The action (second argument to expOutcomesFF) sets the starting disturbances: the agent wants coffee (Want(Co).p), has the ingredients (H(Ings).p), and the combined readiness state s1 is present.

# Initial situation: everything at zero
s <- setNames(rep(0, length(fprops$vnames)), as.vector(fprops$vnames))

# Run the simulation with the four-step plan
sitlog <- expOutcomesFF(
  s,
  i      = c("s1", "Want(Co).p", "H(Ings).p"),
  ffield = ff,
  plan   = do4step
)

# Quick look at the situation matrix (rows = time steps, cols = nodes)
round(sitlog$sit[1:9, ], 2)
#>       s1 make(s2).p  s2 turn(ON.DC).p H(Co).Pot pour(Pot,Cup).p H(Co).Cup
#>  [1,]  1          0 0.0           0.0      0.00            0.00      0.00
#>  [2,]  1          1 0.0           0.0      0.00            0.00      0.00
#>  [3,]  1          1 0.9           0.0      0.00            0.00      0.00
#>  [4,]  1          1 0.9           0.9      0.00            0.00      0.00
#>  [5,]  1          1 0.9           0.9      0.81            0.00      0.00
#>  [6,]  1          1 0.9           0.9      0.81            0.81      0.00
#>  [7,]  1          1 0.9           0.9      0.81            0.81      0.73
#>  [8,]  1          1 0.9           0.9      0.81            0.81      0.73
#>  [9,]  1          1 0.9           0.9      0.81            0.81      0.73
#>       sip(Cup).p H(Co).p U.p Want(Co).p H(Ings).p
#>  [1,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [2,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [3,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [4,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [5,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [6,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [7,]       0.00    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [8,]       0.73    0.00   0          1         1
#>  [9,]       0.73    0.66   0          1         1

Step 5: Visualize with fieldSequenceWidget()

fieldSequenceWidget() renders each time step as a high-resolution image and wraps them in an interactive widget with ← Back and Forward → buttons, so you can step through the simulation one frame at a time. Images are rendered at 2× the display size for sharpness on retina screens.

Here we select the four key steps — the moments just after each new gate opens:

fieldSequenceWidget(
  sitlog, fprops,
  steps       = c(1, 3, 5, 7),
  node_labels = coffee_labels,
  titles = c(
    "Step 1: Gate 1 open \u2014 set up coffeemaker",
    "Step 3: Gate 2 open \u2014 turn on; coffee brews into pot",
    "Step 5: Gate 3 open \u2014 pour pot into cup",
    "Step 7: Gate 4 open \u2014 sip; coffee acquired"
  )
)
Step 1: Gate 1 open — set up coffeemaker
Step 3: Gate 2 open — turn on; coffee brews into pot
Step 5: Gate 3 open — pour pot into cup
Step 7: Gate 4 open — sip; coffee acquired
Step 1 of 4

To step through every simulated time step, omit steps entirely:

fieldSequenceWidget(sitlog, fprops, node_labels = coffee_labels)
Step 1
Step 2
Step 3
Step 4
Step 5
Step 6
Step 7
Step 8
Step 9
Step 10
Step 1 of 10

Interpretation

Each plot shows the active field at that moment. Edges appear only where the affordance gate is open (the prerequisite situation node is positive) and, for planned actions, an intentional impulse is also present:

Step Gate opens when… Path that becomes active
1 s1 present s1 → make(s2).p → s2
3 s2 present s2 → turn(ON.DC).p → H(Co).Pot
5 H(Co).Pot present H(Co).Pot → pour(Pot,Cup).p → H(Co).Cup
7 H(Co).Cup present H(Co).Cup → sip(Cup).p → H(Co).p
9+ H(Co).p present H(Co).p → U.p (terminal utility)

Key takeaway: The staircase layout makes the sequential dependency tangible. Sipping is not merely last in time — it is only causally possible once coffee is in the cup, which requires having poured from the pot, which requires having brewed, which requires setting up the machine. The plan supplies the motivational impulses at each stage, but the affordance structure of the situation controls which actions can have downstream effects at any given moment.
